answer:
**Ghalib (*Diwan-e-Ghalib*, ch. 26):**  
"My pain would not be indebted to a cure;  
I did not heal, nor did my state grow worse."[^1]  

Here *dard* functions as an autonomous, self-perpetuating essence of the lover's being—proudly refusing remedy or change, neither worsening nor improving. An English-only reader loses the *ghazal*'s irony and dignity: *dard* isn't passive suffering but a sovereign state, emblematic of love's unyielding integrity, where healing would betray the self.[^2]

**Zauq (*Diwan-e-Zauq*, ch. 39):**  
"If I had not suppressed my sigh of pain,  
A smoke would rise, a sky of soot and stain  
would be."[^3]  

Here *dard* evokes a barely contained cataclysmic force, its sigh threatening cosmic devastation like a smothered volcano. Rendering it simply "pain" misses the hyperbolic *ishtial* (inner fire) of classical Urdu *ishq*, reducing volcanic passion to a personal whimper instead of world-engulfing turmoil.

**Iqbal (*Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17):**  
"O, Anguish of Love! A pearl of purest water, you;  
Be not revealed to the unworthy view."[^4]  

Personified as *Dard-e-Ishq*, *dard* is a radiant, sacred jewel—spiritual treasure to conceal from a profane world. A plain "pain" erases its Sufi elevation: not torment to escape, but luminous *gauhar* (gem) of divine longing, whose value lies in hidden depth amid modernity's superficiality.[^5]

[^1]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-E-Ghalib*, ch. 26, [p-fd05a7](/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/26-dard-minnat-kash-e-dawa-na-hua/translation/#p-fd05a7).
[^2]: The original Urdu *dard minnat-kash-e-dawa na hua* underscores this stasis as existential fidelity.[^6]
[^3]: Sheikh Ibrahim Zauq, *Diwan-E-Zauq*, ch. 39, [p-1b63cf](/works/sheikh-ibrahim-zauq-diwan-e-zauq-0fa798/39-na-karta-zabt-main-naala-to-phir-aisa-dhuaan-hota/translation/#p-1b63cf).
[^4]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-E-Dara Part 1*, ch. 17, [p-6d19d8](/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/17-dard-e-ishq/translation/#p-6d19d8).
[^5]: The chapter title *Dard-e-Ishq* frames it as love's quintessence, personified throughout.[^7]
[^6]: From chapter wiki key passage.
[^7]: Ibid., full chapter read.
